3.4.1.6. Disadvantaged Communities
Disadvantaged communities (DACs) within the Plan area include, but are not limited to, those served by private domestic wells or small community water systems. Disadvantaged communities generally refer to areas where residents suffer from a combination of economic, health, and environmental burdens that may hinder residents’ ability to engage and participate in SGMA implementation activities. These burdens may include poverty, high unemployment, air and water pollution, presence of hazardous wastes, as well as high incidence of asthma and heart disease. DACs in the Plan area include non-English speaking residents, tribal members, Latinx communities, and economically disadvantaged residents. DACs are defined in the CWC as communities with an annual median household income that is less than 80 percent ($51,026) of the Statewide annual median household income ($63,783) (CWC §79505.5). In addition, the California Public Resources Code defines severely disadvantaged communities (SDAC) as those with an annual MHI less than 60 percent of the of the Statewide annual MHI (Public Resources Code §79505). The CWC §79505.5a also defines economically distressed areas (EDA) as municipalities with a population of 20,000 persons or less, a rural county, or a reasonably isolated and divisible segment of a larger municipality where the segment of the population is 20,000 persons or less, with an annual MHI that is less than 85 percent of the statewide MHI, and with one or more of the following conditions as determined by DWR: 1) financial hardship; 2) unemployment rate at least 2 percent higher than the statewide average; or 3) low population density (CWC §79702(k)). DACs, SDACs, and EDAs, are collectively referenced here as Disadvantaged Areas (DAs). Table 3-5 presents the percentage of area within the Subbasin that are designated as DACs and EDAs.

Described in the NCGSA Communication and Engagement Plan (CONCUR, 2020), NCGSA considers the interests of underrepresented community members within the Subbasin, which includes non-English speaking residents, tribal members, Latinx communities, and disadvantaged communities. The NCGSA uses multiple sources of information to identify disadvantaged residents and map DAs in the Plan area, including maps based on U.S. Census Bureau data provided by DWR, data from County programs and departments, and data from partners and outside groups. Data show that DAs occur throughout the Subbasin, including areas in the northern and central Subbasin and in the City of Napa in the southern Subbasin. An important subset of DAs are SDACs that cover about 2.4% of the Subbasin and include areas near and within the Cities of Calistoga and St. Helena. Figure 3-13 shows the location and extent of DACs and SDACs mapped by DWR, using U.S. Census Bureau data, and farmworker housing centers mapped by the County. Farmworker housing centers are publicly owned centers that primarily serve migrant and temporary farmworkers. No applications from individuals identifying as representing a DAC were received to participate on the GSPAC. To compensate for this absence, NCGSA staff plan to identify the barriers to participation for these groups and devise methods to overcome these barriers. Staff plan to build relationships with social service and other organizations that have connections to these community members to reach them and communicate effectively in English and Spanish. GSA staff will work with Spanish-speaking staff at the NCRCD and will use translation resources available through DWR where possible. While there are no federally recognized tribes within Napa County, there are members of tribes who reside in Napa County. The GSA will encourage the participation of local tribal organizations. The GSA is actively reaching out to the Yocha Dehe Wintun Nation, which is a federally recognized tribe in proximity to Napa County and the Napa Valley Subbasin, to identify whether and how the tribe would like to be involved in GSP development and implementation.

3.4.2. Public Notices and Opportunities for Public Engagement
Consistent with the NCGSA Communication and Engagement Plan (2020), the NCGSA coordinates with stakeholders and conducts outreach and solicitation of input to achieve transparent and stakeholder1849 driven water resource management in the Napa Valley Subbasin. Described in Section 3.4.1, the NCGSA considers the interests of beneficial users within the Subbasin and maintains a list interested parties that the NCGSA uses to inform these individuals of opportunities to participate in the Plan development. The NCGSA posts meeting agendas; all meetings are open to the public and publicized on the Count website. Also on the County website, interested persons may sign up to be on the NCGSA email list to receive SGMA and GSP-related updates. NCGSA will use the list of interested parties to provide notification and information related to implementation of this Plan following its adoption. A total of X public meetings took place during the development of this Plan (Table 3-6). Meetings were structured to address one or more Sections of the GSP and provide progress updates on GSP development. Draft GSP Sections were released according to a publicly posted schedule for public comment beginning in July2020. The release of GSP sections and scheduled meetings were staggered to provide time for stakeholder review of GSP sections and to allow for a question and comment period at subsequent public meetings. To encourage the active involvement of social, cultural, and economic elements within the Subbasin boundaries, public meetings were held to inform the public of the status of GSP development and how the public could be involved in the process. The NCGSA will continue to inform the public regarding the progress of Plan implementation on its website (https://www.countyofnapa.org/1238/Groundwater1866 Sustainability-Planning).

3.4.1.4. Environmental Users of Groundwater
Environmental users of groundwater within the Plan area include GDEs in the Subbasin and species that rely on interconnected surface waters. Additionally, environmental users of groundwater and interconnected surface water include entities that represent the interests of environmental users of groundwater, such as CDFW, USFW, NOAA Fisheries, and non-governmental organizations. The interests of environmental users of groundwater and interconnected surface water are represented through members of the GSPAC that are members of or otherwise associated with several groups, including the Sierra Club, Water Audit California, the Napa County Resource Conservation District, and other organizations. The NCGSA reviewed guidance documents and reference materials provided the stakeholder groups including TNC, CDFW, NOAA Fisheries, University of California at Davis, and Audubon to inform mapping of GDEs within the Subbasin and to identify particular species known to be groundwater dependent for all or part of their life cycle. (Matsumoto, 2019, Klausmeyer et al., 2019, and Rhode et al., 2019). 
Location information indicating the distribution of environmental users of groundwater, including potential GDEs and groundwater dependent freshwater species, show that they are present throughout the Subbasin, including the Napa River and many of its tributaries (Figure 3-12). Through outreach to state and federal resources agencies and a review of reference materials, including an excerpt of the California Freshwater Species Database for species identified in the Napa Valley Subbasin, the NCGSA identified 12 potentially groundwater dependent freshwater species and nine additional species of special concern (Table 3-4). Species identification is an initial step towards considering the reliance on groundwater by environmental users of groundwater. Additional information about the distribution of GDEs and environmental users of groundwater is provided in Section 6. Additional information about the potential effects of Subbasin management on these beneficial users is presented in Section 9. Projects and management actions to avoid significant and unreasonable effects on beneficial users are presented in Section 11.

General Groundwater Quality
This section provides a generalized description of groundwater quality in the Plan area for the purposes of the HCM. A detailed discussion of groundwater quality conditions is provided in Section 6.1.3.  Groundwater quality in the alluvial formations of the Subbasin is generally suitable for most beneficial uses (DWR, 2003). Groundwater quality in the Sonoma Volcanics formations is also generally good, with ​relatively low concentrations of chloride, sulfate, calcium, and magnesium (DWR, 2003). Localize ​exceptions have been documented, including elevated iron and sulfur concentrations near the MS ​Subarea and elevated boron concentrations that exceed criteria for agricultural irrigation (Johnson ​1977). In the Calistoga Subarea, elevated concentrations of iron, boron, and total dissolved solids have ​been documented in geothermal groundwater (Taylor et al., 1981). Some wells near and south of the ​City of Napa also exhibit elevated chloride concentrations, possibly due to seepage from brackish water ​in the Napa River (Kunkel and Upson, 1960)

Total Dissolved Solids
Maps of historical and recent maximum TDS concentrations in groundwater for select time periods in the Napa Valley Subbasin are shown in Figure 6-20 to Figure 6-23 and indicate relatively low TDS concentrations in groundwater quality across most of the Subbasin, with maximum historical TDS concentrations less than 500 mg/L in a majority of the region. Wells with historical TDS concentrations greater than the SMCL of 500 mg/L exist in the northern end of the Subbasin north of Calistoga, and in the southern end of the Subbasin south of Napa (SWRCB, 2018). Elevated TDS concentrations in the northern Calistoga area can be associated with known hydrothermal waters and possible faults that are known to source groundwater containing elevated concentrations of dissolved minerals in the western Coast Range region (Faye, 1973). South of Napa, several wells with high TDS levels occur near the Napa River and its marshes. High TDS concentrations at these wells are presumably sourced from brackish reaches of the Napa River or draw connate groundwater from Tertiary units below the alluvium (Kunkel and Upson, 1960). Average TDS concentrations are greatest in the Napa River Marshes Subarea, measuring approximately 4,126 mg/L, whereas average TDS concentrations in the remaining subareas measure at or below 305 mg/L (Appendix 6G). Maximum TDS concentrations for the total period of available data show three wells within the Subbasin that have exceeded concentrations greater than 1,000 mg/L, all of which are located within the Napa Subarea (Figure 6-24).

Chloride
Maps of historical and recent maximum chloride concentrations in groundwater for select time periods are shown in Figure 6-25 to Figure 6-28. Historical chloride concentrations for the time periods from 1958 to 1963 and 1985 to 1990 indicate elevated levels north of Calistoga, south of Napa, and in the center of the valley; however, these levels do not exceed the SMCL. Conversely, recent chloride concentrations from 2010 to 2020 show SMCL exceedances south of the City of Napa and within the city limits of Napa near the tidally-influenced reach of the Napa River. Average chloride concentrations exceeding the SMCL are greatest in the Napa River Marshes Subarea, measuring approximately 575 mg/L (Appendix 6G). Chloride measurements in the Napa River Marshes Subarea range from 4 mg/L to 3,900 mg/L, respectively (Appendix 6G). In the remaining subareas, chloride concentrations range from less than 2 mg/L to 350 mg/L in the Napa Subarea, 2 mg/L to 200 mg/L in the Calistoga Subarea, 2 mg/L to 175 mg/L in the MST Subarea, 4 mg/L to 151 mg/L in the St. Helena Subarea, and 4 mg/L to 48 mg/L in the Yountville Subarea (Appendix 6G). Maximum chloride concentrations for the total period of available data show nine wells within the Subbasin that have exceeded concentrations greater than 500 mg/L, all of which are located within the southern area of the valley within the Napa and MST Subareas (Figure 6-29).

 Nitrate
Maps of historical and recent maximum nitrate (as nitrogen) concentrations in groundwater for select time periods are shown in Figure 6-30 to Figure 6-33. Most wells with nitrate results have nitrate-N concentrations that are less than 5 mg/L, however, nitrate concentrations exceeding 10 mg/L have been measured in wells in the Napa River Marshes, MST, Napa, and Yountville Subareas. Although nitrate concentrations are generally within acceptable limits throughout the Subbasin, concentrations are typically higher in agricultural areas of the valley. Except for three wells in the Napa Subarea, the majority of recent nitrate concentrations in wells throughout the Subbasin have measured less than 5 mg/L (Figure 6-32 and Figure 6-33). Average nitrate (as nitrogen) concentrations in the MST Subarea are approximately 4 mg/L. Nitrate concentrations in the Napa Valley Subbasin range from non-detected to 52 mg/L in the Napa River Marshes Subarea, non-detected to 8.8 mg/L in the Calistoga and St. Helena Subareas, non-detected to 10 mg/L in the MST Subarea, non-detected to 11 mg/L in the Napa Subarea, and non-detected to 11 mg/L in the Yountville Subarea (Appendix 6G). Maximum nitrate concentrations for the total period of available data show 14 wells within the Subbasin that have exceeded concentrations greater than 10 mg/L (Figure 6-34). Two of these wells are located in the Yountville Subarea, three are in the Napa Subarea, and nine are in the MST Subarea.

Arsenic
Maps of historical and recent maximum arsenic concentrations in groundwater for select time periods are shown in Figure 6-35 to Figure 6-37. Wells with arsenic concentrations greater than the primary MCL of 10 μg/L are scattered throughout the Subbasin. Elevated arsenic concentrations in the central and southern portions of the Subbasin are primarily the result of arsenic-releasing anoxic conditions that occur during the dissolution of iron and manganese oxides (Kulongoski and others, 2010). Recent arsenic concentrations that exceed the MCL are still present throughout the Subbasin in several of the same wells that historically exceeded the MCL. Average arsenic concentrations are greatest in the Yountville and Calistoga Subareas, measuring 102 μg/L and 28 μg/L, respectively, whereas average concentrations in the remaining subareas all measure below 10 μg/L (Appendix 6G). Arsenic concentrations in the Napa Valley Subbasin range from non-detected to 100 μg/L in the Napa River Marshes Subarea, non-detected to 220 μg/L in the Calistoga Subarea, non-detected to 86 μg/L in the MST Subarea, non-detected to 47 μg/L in the Napa Subarea, non-detected to 124 μg/L in the St. Helena Subarea, and non-detected to 880 μg/L in the Yountville Subarea (Appendix 6G). Maximum arsenic concentrations for the total period of available data show 52 wells throughout the Subbasin that have exceeded concentrations greater than 10 μg/L (Figure 6-38).

Iron 
Maps of historical and recent maximum iron concentrations in groundwater for select time periods are shown in Figure 6-39 to Figure 6-42. Historical data for the designated time periods from 1958 to 1963 and 1985 to 1990 are limited, however, recent data show 5 wells have exceeded the SMCL. Two of these sites are located in the Calistoga Subarea and the remainder are located in the Yountville and MST8 Subareas. Average iron concentrations are greatest in the Calistoga Subarea, measuring approximately 4,915 μg/L, followed by Yountville, Napa River Marshes, St. Helena, Napa, and MST subareas (Appendix 6G). Maximum iron concentrations for the total period of available data show 40 wells throughout the Subbasin that have exceeded concentrations greater than 300 μg/L (Figure 6-43).

Manganese
Maps of historical and recent maximum manganese concentrations in groundwater for select time periods are shown in Figure 6-44 to Figure 6-46. Historical data from 1985 to 1990 are limited, however, recent data starting from 2010 show 7 wells have exceeded the SMCL. Three of the recent exceedances have been measured the Calistoga Subarea, three in the St. Helena Subarea, and one in the MST Subarea. Average manganese concentrations are greatest in the Napa River Marshes Subarea, measuring approximately 1,035 μg/L, following by Calistoga Subarea at 450 μg/L, Yountville Subarea at 322 μg/L, Napa Subarea at 293 μg/L, St. Helena Subarea at 242 μg/L, and MST Subarea at 57 μg/L, respectively. Maximum manganese concentrations for the total period of available data show 65 wells throughout the Subbasin that have exceeded concentrations greater than 50 μg/L (Figure 6-47 and Appendix 6G).

Other Constituents
Other groundwater quality constituents of concern include boron (Figure 6-48 to Figure 6-52), sodium (Figure 6-53 to Figure 6-57), and sulfate (Figure 6-58 to Figure 6-62). Currently, boron is an unregulated chemical without an established MCL, however, the California State Notification Level (CA-NL) is 1,000 μg/L, and there is an agricultural goal of 700 μg/L. Boron in drinking water is often attributed from leaching of rocks and soils, wastewater, and fertilizers/pesticides. Average boron concentrations are greatest in the Calistoga and MST Subareas, measuring approximately 4,600 μg/L and 1,100 μg/L, respectively. Boron concentrations range from non-detected to 14,000 μg/L in the Calistoga Subarea and non-detected to 11,000 μg/L in the MST Subarea (Appendix 6G). Average sodium concentrations measured throughout the Subbasin range from approximately 26 mg/L in the Yountville Subarea to 155 mg/L in the Napa River Marshes Subarea, respectively (Appendix 6G). Sodium does not have an established primary or secondary MCL, however, it has a Federal Action Level of 50 mg/L and an agricultural goal of 69 mg/L. Groundwater typically contains higher concentrations of sodium than surface waters due to its abundance and high solubility of sodium mineral deposits. Anthropogenic sources of sodium include water treatment chemicals, domestic water softeners, and sewage effluents. Sulfate has a recommended California SMCL of 250 mg/L, with an upper limit of 500 mg/L. In the Napa Valley Subbasin, average sulfate concentrations range from 15 mg/L in the Calistoga Subarea to 500 mg/L in the Napa River Marshes Subarea (Appendix 6G). Sulfate is a naturally occurring substance present in ambient air, groundwater, plants, and food. Commercial use of sulfate in the chemical  industry is the primary anthropogenic source, often discharged to surface waters through industrial wastes.
&&&
6.3.3.4. Pesticides
Dibromochloropropane (DBCP) and 1,2,3-Trichloropropane (1,2,3-TCP) are common compounds associated with pesticide use, although 1,2,3-TCP has been used in limited industrial applications as a paint remover. DBCP and 1,2,3-TCP were both used as fumigants to kill nematodes in soils until discontinued in 1979 and 1984, respectively. The primary MCLs of DBCP and 1,2,3-TCP are 0.20 μg/L and 0.005 μg/L, respectively. Maps of maximum concentrations of DBCP and 1,2,3-TCP in the Napa Valley Subbasin (including the MST Subarea) are shown in Figure 6-63 to Figure 6-67. From 2010 to 2015, six wells scattered throughout the Subbasin measured DBCP concentrations exceeding the primary MCL (Figure 6-63). Since 2015, however, 64 wells have been measured for DBCP; zero MCL exceedances were recorded during this period (Figure 6-64). Maximum DBCP concentrations for the total period of available data show 15 wells with recorded MCL exceedances (Figure 6-65). Maximum 1,2,3-TCP concentrations from 2010 to 2015 show MCL exceedances in 11 wells scattered throughout the Subbasin (Figure 6-66). From 2015 to 2020, maximum recorded 1,2,3-TCP concentrations exceeded the MCL in 24 wells (Figure 6-67). Maximum 1,2,3-TCP concentrations for the total period of available data show 40 wells with recorded MCL exceedances (Figure 6-68).
&&&
6.3.3.5. Petroleum Hydrocarbons
Several active remediation sites within the Subbasin, denoted in Table 6-5, are contaminated by petroleum fuels such as gasoline, diesel, and motor oil. Of the compounds that make up these petroleum products, benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, xylenes (BTEX) and methyl-tert-butyl ether (MTBE) are the primary hydrocarbons used to summarize petroleum contamination in the Napa Valley Subbasin. The four Alkyl Benzene volatile organic compounds included in BTEX are common constituents of petroleum fuels and serve as an indicator parameter representing the wide variety of compounds found in petroleum products. The standard MCL for each compound is: 1 μg/L for benzene; 150 μg/L for toluene; 300 μg/L for ethylbenzene; and 1,750 μg/L for total xylenes. Historical maximum BTEX concentrations between 1985 to 1990 show no standard exceedances (Figure 6-69), however, maximum concentrations between 2010 and 2020 show standard exceedances throughout the Subbasin (Figure 6- 70 and Figure 6-71). A majority of these BTEX exceedances are located in or around the city limits of Calistoga, St. Helena, Yountville, and Napa. Elevated BTEX concentrations are also found in the southernmost portion of the Subbasin. Well sites with measured BTEX exceedances are all associated with a regulated GeoTracker remediation/cleanup site (Figure 6-72). MTBE is a regulated contaminant with an established MCL of 13 785 μg/L and an SMCL of 5 μg/L. MTBE is a flammable liquid that was commonly used as a gasoline additive until its prohibition in 2004. Less common applications of MTBE include laboratory solvent and some medical uses. There are no recorded measurements of MTBE for the time periods from 1958 to 1963 and from 1985 to 1990. However, measurements from 2010 to 2020 show MTBE standard exceedances at a majority of sites with concurrent BTEX exceedances (Figure 6-73 and Figure 6-74). Between 2010 to 2015, 56 wells recorded MTBE concentrations greater than the MCL; whereas, from 2015 to 2020, 9 wells recorded MTBE concentrations exceeding the MCL. Well sites with measured MTBE exceedances are all associated with a regulated GeoTracker remediation/cleanup site.
&&&
6.3.3.6. Solvents
Maximum concentrations of 1,1,1- Trichloroethane (111-TCA), Tetrachloroethene (PCE), and Trichloroethene (TCE) are used to summarize the extent of solvent contamination within the Napa Valley Subbasin. PCE and TCE are volatile organic compounds (VOCs) commonly associated with discharges related to dry cleaning operations and metal degreasing processes and listed as potential cancer-causing agents. Additionally, TCE is listed as a reproductive toxin causing developmental toxicity and has the potential to enter groundwater in the form of a dense non-aqueous liquid (DNAPL). The MCL of PCE and TCE is established at 5 μg/L. Between 1985 to 1990, zero detections of PCE and TCE were found in groundwater (Figure 6-76 and Figure 6-80). From 2010 to 2015, however, MCL exceedances occurred in 11 wells for PCE and in 9 wells for TCE (Figure 6-77 and Figure 6-81). PCE and TCE MCL exceedances are concentrated within the city limits of Napa and in the southernmost portion of the Napa Valley Subbasin. TCE exceedances from 2015 to 2020 occur at four GeoTracker regulated sites (Figure 6-82), in which three of the sites are concurrent with the locations of PCE exceedances from 2010 to 2020 as well (Figure 6-78). 111-TCA is a VOC known to cause cancer and has been classified as an emerging contaminant by the SWRCB. 111-TCA has an established MCL of 200 μg/L. Maximum 111-TCA concentrations from 1985 to 1990 show zero standard exceedances or detections (Figure 6-84). Between 2010 to 2015, a single well in the southernmost area of the Napa Valley Subbasin measured a maximum concentration exceeding the MCL (Figure 6-85). From 2015 to 2020, however, the same well recorded zero detections (Figure 6- 86) and is the only well to have a recorded concentration exceedance over the period of available data (Figure 6-87).
&&&
6.3.3.7. Hexavalent Chromium
Chromium VI is considered toxic, in which inhalation or ingestion of chromium VI is known to cause cancer. In 2014, the SWRCB established the MCL for chromium VI as 10 μg/L, however, the MCL was withdrawn in 2017. Until a revised MCL is adopted by the SWRCB, the MCL for total chromium (50 μg/L) will be applied as the drinking water standard from chromium VI. In addition to the MCL set by the SWRCB, the USGS established a health-based screening level (HBSL, non-cancer health effects) of 20 μg/L. Concentrations over select historical and recent time periods show the detection of chromium VI is common throughout the Napa Valley Subbasin (Figure 6-88 to Figure 6-90). Maximum chromium VI concentrations for the total period of available data show zero exceedances of the MCL and HBSL for total chromium and chromium VI, although chromium VI has been detected at concentrations ranging from non-detect levels to 10 μg/L in the MST Subarea (Figure 6-91 and Appendix 6G).

Interactions between groundwater and surface water in the Subbasin are temporally and spatially variable and, as in many settings, challenging to quantify. This Plan relies on many data types to evaluate the extent and degree of groundwater and surface water interconnections and rates of streamflow depletion. These include: - Groundwater elevation monitoring data, - Stream elevation and discharge rate data, - Streamflow rates modeled by the USGS Basin Characterization Model (Flint and Flint, 2014), - GDE location data, - Remote-sensing datasets characterizing GDE water use, - Reported deliveries of surface water from sources outside of the Subbasin, - Projected deliveries of surface water to the Subbasin that incorporate the effects of climate change, - Projected inflows of surface water from the Napa River Watershed that incorporate the effects of climate change, and - Projected sea level rise due to climate change.

&& Surface waters with a perennial hydraulic connection to groundwater in the Subbasin include perennial reaches of the Napa River and its tributaries (Figure 4-29). Surface waters with an intermittent hydraulic connection to groundwater in the Subbasin include some intermittent reaches of the Napa River and its tributaries. Reaches of some tributaries, including Dry Creek from the Subbasin margin to at least Highway 29, may be hydraulically disconnected from groundwater (SFEI, 2012; LSCE, 2020). Additional information about surface waters hydraulically connected to groundwater in the Subbasin is presented in Section 6 of this Plan.

&& Notable surface waters in the Subbasin include the Napa River and its tributaries, including Bale Slough, Dry Creek, Conn Creek, Garnett Creek, Mill Creek, Napa Creek, Rector Creek, Redwood Creek, Ritchie Creek, Soda Creek, Sulphur Creek, Tulucay Creek, and York Creek (Figure 6-93). Each of these streams is considered to have a hydraulic connection to groundwater. Some streams are perennial reaches of the Napa River and others are intermittent tributaries, which only have an established connection to groundwater during specific periods of the year.

&& A study of spring 2010 groundwater levels compared to Napa River thalweg (deepest portion of a stream channel) elevations indicate surface water – groundwater interactions are prevalent along the mainstem of surveyed areas included in the study (Figure 6-118) (LSCE, 2013). Calculated depths to groundwater below the estimated thalweg alignment are commonly “negative” for Spring 2010 indicating that the interpreted groundwater elevation was above the bottom of the Napa River thalweg. These negative values suggest areas where a direct connection between the water table and the river may have existed in Spring 2010 and where groundwater has the potential to discharge into the stream channel. These areas include reaches along the northern boundary of the Napa and MST Subareas at the Soda Creek Fault, adjacent to a previously documented area of lower groundwater elevations. In other areas, the depth to groundwater was below the bottom of the Napa River thalweg such that surface flows in the river have the potential to percolate and recharge the groundwater system. Additional maps representing groundwater elevations during spring 2015 and spring 2019 in relation to the Napa River thalweg are shown in Figure 6-119 and Figure 6-120. These snapshots of spring 2010, 2015, and 2019 conditions indicate the extent of groundwater connection with the Napa River thalweg is spatially variable, likely dependent on wet and dry year conditions.

&& Continuous monitoring of groundwater-surface water connection occurs at five dedicated monitoring sites in the Subbasin, with four additional sites planned to be constructed in 2021. At each site stream stage is monitored concurrently with shallow and deep groundwater levels in the alluvial aquifer. The locations of these wells are shown in Figure 5-8, represented as dedicated monitoring sites. Data collected from these sites indicate shallow groundwater and surface waters are typically hydraulically connected throughout much of the winter and spring at the mainstem Napa River sites; interconnectivity is longer in some locations (Figure 6-14 to Figure 6-18). The direction of flow indicated by monitoring data varied between gaining stream (flow of groundwater into surface water) and losing stream (flow of surface water into the groundwater system) conditions at most sites.

The formations of the Quaternary surficial deposits comprise the principal aquifer system in the Subbasin (Faye, 1973, LSCE and MBK, 2013). As noted in this Plan, many production wells in the Subbasin extend beyond the alluvium and into the underlying Tertiary units, which may also warrant designation as principal aquifers. Available information compiled for this Plan indicates that 76 percent of production wells in the Subbasin have screened intervals completely or partially within the Quaternary surficial deposits, while 24 percent of production wells are screened entirely within older Tertiary units, including the Huichica formation and Sonoma Volcanics. The characterization of principal aquifers presented in this Plan will be revised to reflect calibrated results from the Napa Valley Integrated Hydrologic Model (NVIHM) under development as part of Plan development. Model results indicating significant quantities of production from Tertiary units will lead to inclusion of on or more of those formations as principal aquifers designated by this Plan.

Approximately 6.3% of the Napa Valley Subbasin is covered by GDEs, in which 5.8% 1363 is classified as GDE vegetation and 0.50% is classified as GDE wetland. GDE vegetation within the Subbasin includes groups and species such as Valley Oak (California Bay - Coast Live Oak - Walnut - Ash Riparian Forest), Coast Live Oak Alliance, Bulrush – Cattail, Fremont Cottonwood, Valley Oak Alliance, Mixed Willow Super Alliance, and White Alder (Mixed Willow - California Bay - Big Leaf Maple Riparian Forest). GDE wetlands within the Subbasin include palustrine, riverine, and natural seeps and springs. Table 6-9 summarizes the acreages of each of these GDE types within the Subbasin.

&& In addition to GDE areas, the Napa Valley Subbasin coincides with approximately 230 acres of critical habitat for the Contra Costa Goldfields (Lasthenia conjugens) and approximately 23 miles of critical habitat for the Central California Coast Steelhead (Oncorhynchus mykiss - 1394 CCC winter) within the Subbasin’s waterways (Figure 3-12). Critical habitat for the Contra Costa Goldfields is located at the very southern end of the Subbasin, where the plant is generally found in vernal pools within grassy areas in woodlands and valley grasslands. The Contra Costa Goldfields is a federally listed endangered species given special status in California. Lastly, the Central California Coast Steelhead is a threatened species commonly associated with native vegetation riparian species and is considered to have a direct reliance on groundwater (Appendix 6H). The areas of critical habitat delineated for these two species are taken into particular consideration in the Subbasin’s account of GDEs. A more detailed description of the ecological conditions for these critical habitats within the Subbasin are provided in the following section

Where present, Endangered Species Act critical habitat is described as are groundwater conditions from shallow wells and long-term trends in GDE vegetation metrics.

GDEs exist where vegetation can access shallow groundwater for survival. In accordance with GDE identification from Rohde et al. (2019), GDEs are likely found in areas where groundwater is within 30 feet from the ground surface, therefore, iGDE polygons located in areas where groundwater occurs greater than 30 feet from the surface can be removed from the dataset. Unnamed: 9:
Three primary projected water budget scenarios are detailed below: 1) Scenario A, a projected baseline scenario, 2) Scenario B, a Wet/Warm climate scenario, and 3) Scenario C, a Warm/Multi-Decade drought scenario. These projected water budgets consider the impacts of climate change and future projections of land use and population. Table 8-16 summarizes the conditions evaluated within the future scenarios, extending through 2070.
Scenario A is considered the projected baseline scenario because it utilizes hydrologic conditions from the historical and current (baseline) periods, which runs from 1988 to 2019. However, Scenario A models conditions from 1969 to 2019, which translates to a future projection from 2020 to 2070. Scenarios B and C utilize climate data provided by DWR in which perturbations in predicted climate result in variable hydrologic responses. Scenario B utilizes the CNRM-CM5 with RCP 4.5 climate model to represent relatively wetter with moderate warming conditions, while Scenario C utilizes the HadGEM2-ES with RCP 8.5 climate model to represent relatively drier with extreme warming conditions. In an assessment of global climate change models comparing 2070-2099 versus 1961-1990 projections, annual temperature increased by 6.7°F in the CNRM-CM5 model and by 10.9°F in the HadGEM2-ES model. Annual precipitation increased by 7.9 inches in the CNRM-CM5 model whereas precipitation decreased by 0.40 inches in the HadGEM2-ES model. Per recommendations from DWR, GSAs are encouraged to model future conditions using the CNRM-CM5 and HadGEM2-ES climate models that that represent more stressful conditions than other climate models provided by DWR.

Sustainable yield was quantified and evaluated using historical and projected groundwater pumping, changes in groundwater storage summarized in Section 8. Estimates were also compared with respect to MTs developed for the Subbasin and described in Section 9.5.

Projected groundwater extraction and storage were compared in the Baseline and HadGEM2-ES scenarios (Table 9-23). Projected groundwater pumping in the Baseline scenario ranges from 15,400 AFY in the early projected period (2020-2040) and 15,800 in the later projected period (2041-2070). Groundwater storage during the early period shows an average annual decrease, while the later period shows an annual average increase. Projected groundwater pumping in the HadGEM2-ES scenario ranges from 15,300 AFY (2020-2040) and 16,700 (2041-2070). Groundwater storage during the early period shows an average annual increase, while the later period shows an annual average decrease. Groundwater extraction and changes in groundwater storage were used to develop preliminary estimates of sustainable yield. Analysis of groundwater levels in conjunction with modeling suggest that the Subbasin is not experiencing chronic overdraft in the historical or projected periods. However, in order to estimate the amount of pumping that can occur while maintaining sustainable groundwater storage conditions, the groundwater pumping within each period was adjusted based on the commensurate change in groundwater storage to develop preliminary estimates of sustainable yield shown in Table 9-23.

Unnamed: 9:
9.5.1.1 Undesirable Results (§354.26)
Significant and unreasonable chronic lowering of groundwater levels specific to the Napa Valley Subbasin occur when either one of the following two conditions materialize: Condition 1: If groundwater conditions in the Napa Valley Subbasin result in prolonged, year-to year reductions in groundwater levels below levels recorded historically [10 years] at representative monitoring wells in the Subbasin, excluding groundwater level declines that may occur during drought conditions, and conditions result in impacts to beneficial uses and users of
 groundwater. Condition 2: Groundwater level declines continue, including during drought periods, extend for a long period [e.g., 10 years], result in impacts to beneficial uses and users of groundwater, and two other conditions apply: a. the long-term period does not end in drought conditions and b. the long-term period includes a balance of above average and below average water years.
&&&
An Undesirable Result because of chronic lowering of groundwater levels 204 occurs if 20% of designated Representative Monitoring Site (RMS) well levels fall below the MT in fall (October) for three consecutive years of fall measurements in non-drought years.
&&&
9.5.1.1.2 Potential Causes of Undesirable Results
Conditions that may lead to undesirable chronic lowering of groundwater level results are: • Significantly drier hydrologic conditions than currently projected under climate change scenarios, causing a significant reduction in natural recharge (Section 8) • Significant changes in groundwater pumping volume and distribution in the Subbasin • Regulatory changes in streamflow requirements imposed by the SWRCB 9.5.1.1.3 Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
The potential effects from chronic lowering of groundwater levels are the potential impact to domestic and agricultural well owners whose wells may go dry, and groundwater dependent ecosystems (GDEs) by reducing the groundwater table available to the plant root zone. These changes could impact property values, quality of life, and the local ecosystems in the Subbasin.
&&&
9.5.4 Degraded Water Quality
9.5.4.1 Undesirable Results (§354.26)
Significant and unreasonable degraded water quality occurs when there is a statistically significant increase in concentrations of groundwater quality constituents of concern that negatively affects the  suitability of groundwater for domestic, agricultural, municipal or environmental beneficial uses over the planning and implementation horizon of this GSP at any RMS in the Napa Valley Subbasin, as a result of either the action or inaction of the GSA with respect to basin management or GSA Projects and Management Actions (PMAs) (Section 11).
 9.5.4.1.1 Criteria Defining Undesirable Results
 An Undesirable Result from degraded water quality occurs when a detected and confirmed exceedance of a MT at any RMS, where the exceedance of the MT is determined to be the result of groundwater conditions caused by action or inaction of the GSA with respect to basin management or PMAs.
9.5.4.1.2 Potential Causes of Undesirable Results
Conditions that may lead to undesirable results for degraded water quality include the following:• Changes in groundwater pumping patterns: Changes to the location or rates of pumping could result in mobilization and migration of certain constituents in the alluvial aquifer including saline water and anthropogenic sources of contamination or natural constituents of concern. • Changes in intensity of groundwater recharge: Changes to the location or intensity (e.g., rate and volume) of recharge that could increase mobilization and migration in the alluvial aquifer of anthropogenic sources of contamination or natural constituents of concern.
9.5.4.1.3 Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
The potential effects of undesirable results for degradation of water quality are the same as those described above for seawater intrusion. The potential effects of undesirable results for degraded water quality are: • Reduced available water supply requiring users to replace wells or seek alternative sources of supply. • Create economic hardships on domestic wells users, many of which reside in DACs, to install water treatment or seek alternative sources. • Added costs to municipalities to install treatment systems or seek alternate sources. • Reduced groundwater quantity and quality for agricultural supply. • Adverse effects to groundwater dependent ecosystems due to changes in freshwater quantity (e.g., outflow) and/or quality. Adverse effects on property values for landowners that rely on groundwater for domestic and agricultural supply. • Reduced crop yields and crop damage from application of groundwater with poor quality.

9.5.1.1 Undesirable Results (§354.26)
Significant and unreasonable chronic lowering of groundwater levels specific to the Napa Valley Subbasin occur when either one of the following two conditions materialize: Condition 1: If groundwater conditions in the Napa Valley Subbasin result in prolonged, year-to year reductions in groundwater levels below levels recorded historically [10 years] at representative monitoring wells in the Subbasin, excluding groundwater level declines that may occur during drought conditions, and conditions result in impacts to beneficial uses and users of
 groundwater. Condition 2: Groundwater level declines continue, including during drought periods, extend for a long period [e.g., 10 years], result in impacts to beneficial uses and users of groundwater, and two other conditions apply: a. the long-term period does not end in drought conditions and b. the long-term period includes a balance of above average and below average water years.
&&&
An Undesirable Result because of chronic lowering of groundwater levels 204 occurs if 20% of designated Representative Monitoring Site (RMS) well levels fall below the MT in fall (October) for three consecutive years of fall measurements in non-drought years.
&&&
9.5.1.1.2 Potential Causes of Undesirable Results
Conditions that may lead to undesirable chronic lowering of groundwater level results are: • Significantly drier hydrologic conditions than currently projected under climate change scenarios, causing a significant reduction in natural recharge (Section 8) • Significant changes in groundwater pumping volume and distribution in the Subbasin • Regulatory changes in streamflow requirements imposed by the SWRCB 9.5.1.1.3 Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
The potential effects from chronic lowering of groundwater levels are the potential impact to domestic and agricultural well owners whose wells may go dry, and groundwater dependent ecosystems (GDEs) by reducing the groundwater table available to the plant root zone. These changes could impact property values, quality of life, and the local ecosystems in the Subbasin.
&&&
9.5.4 Degraded Water Quality
9.5.4.1 Undesirable Results (§354.26)
Significant and unreasonable degraded water quality occurs when there is a statistically significant increase in concentrations of groundwater quality constituents of concern that negatively affects the  suitability of groundwater for domestic, agricultural, municipal or environmental beneficial uses over the planning and implementation horizon of this GSP at any RMS in the Napa Valley Subbasin, as a result of either the action or inaction of the GSA with respect to basin management or GSA Projects and Management Actions (PMAs) (Section 11).
 9.5.4.1.1 Criteria Defining Undesirable Results
 An Undesirable Result from degraded water quality occurs when a detected and confirmed exceedance of a MT at any RMS, where the exceedance of the MT is determined to be the result of groundwater conditions caused by action or inaction of the GSA with respect to basin management or PMAs.
9.5.4.1.2 Potential Causes of Undesirable Results
Conditions that may lead to undesirable results for degraded water quality include the following:• Changes in groundwater pumping patterns: Changes to the location or rates of pumping could result in mobilization and migration of certain constituents in the alluvial aquifer including saline water and anthropogenic sources of contamination or natural constituents of concern. • Changes in intensity of groundwater recharge: Changes to the location or intensity (e.g., rate and volume) of recharge that could increase mobilization and migration in the alluvial aquifer of anthropogenic sources of contamination or natural constituents of concern.
9.5.4.1.3 Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
The potential effects of undesirable results for degradation of water quality are the same as those described above for seawater intrusion. The potential effects of undesirable results for degraded water quality are: • Reduced available water supply requiring users to replace wells or seek alternative sources of supply. • Create economic hardships on domestic wells users, many of which reside in DACs, to install water treatment or seek alternative sources. • Added costs to municipalities to install treatment systems or seek alternate sources. • Reduced groundwater quantity and quality for agricultural supply. • Adverse effects to groundwater dependent ecosystems due to changes in freshwater quantity (e.g., outflow) and/or quality.
&&&
9.5.6.1 Undesirable Results (§354.26)
Significant and unreasonable depletions of interconnected surface water occur when either one of the following two conditions materialize because of groundwater extraction and use in the Subbasin: Condition 1: The timing and duration of direct hydraulic connections between groundwater and surface water along the Napa River or its tributaries overlying the Subbasin are reduced relative to the historical conditions and impact groundwater dependent ecosystems or other beneficial users of surface water, Condition 2: The volume of surface water flowing in the Napa River and/or its tributaries overlying the Subbasin is reduced relative to the historical flow or impacts groundwater dependent ecosystems or other beneficial users of surface water.
9.5.6.1.1 Criteria Defining Undesirable Results
An Undesirable Result because of depletions of interconnected surface waters occurs when 20% of RMS well levels are below the MT in the Fall for three consecutive years of Fall measurements. An interim Undesirable Result is considered to be an exceedance of the MT for the volume of streamflow depletion occurring for three consecutive years at the Napa River at Pope Street or Napa
River at Oak Knoll Avenue locations.
9.5.6.1.2 Potential Causes of Undesirable Results
Potential causes of depletion of interconnected surface water include the following: • New large-scale pumping or diversions from shallow wells. • New localized pumping in the alluvium from wells located in the vicinity of interconnected streams. • Interception or reduction of natural patterns of groundwater discharge to surface water.
9.5.6.1.3 Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
Depletions of interconnected surface water could result in: • Reduction in flows negatively impacting aquatic species and groundwater dependent ecosystems. • Reduced flows within rivers and streams adversely impacting diversions for agricultural or urban users. • Increased costs to mitigate impacts on interconnected surface waters and groundwater dependent ecosystems.

9.5.1.2.1 Methodology
Information used to establish the MT for the chronic lowering of groundwater levels includes: • Historical groundwater elevations within the Subbasin. • Depths and locations of existing wells. • Current and historical groundwater elevation contour maps. • Modeling scenarios for Subbasin sustainable yield including climate change. • Other Information from GSAs and interested parties regarding significant and unreasonable conditions.
Groundwater trends and conditions in the Napa Valley Subbasin are largely dependent upon precipitation inputs; therefore, groundwater levels are reviewed in the context of seasonality (spring and fall) and water year types. Overall, groundwater generally flows through the length of the Subbasin from Calistoga east and south to Napa. Groundwater level trends in the Napa Valley Subbasin are stable in the majority of wells with long-term groundwater level records. Groundwater levels at two wells in the Northeast Napa Management Area (see Section 9.6) have shown declines of 10 to 20 feet since 2000, with stabilization since about 2009. With the exception of the Northeast Napa Management Area, groundwater levels in the Subbasin are generally more stable in wells completed in thicker alluvial deposits near the center of the valley axis than in wells near the Subbasin margin. Wells near the Subbasin margins tend to be constructed in relatively thinner alluvial deposits and draw water from older, deeper formations and exhibit greater groundwater level variability between the spring and fall. The MT for chronic lowering of groundwater levels at each RMS well is set at an elevation that, when evaluated collectively, could produce undesirable results in the Subbasin if: • The MT for each RMP is set at a level for which the sustainable yield is exceeded based on groundwater flow model scenarios. • Where chronic level declines do not exceed the sustainable yield, but otherwise cause undesirable results as described in this GSP. • For domestic wells, an MT which indicates that the 5th percentile of this category experiences a drop below the top perforations within the section where the RMS is located. This is considered protective of the water supply reliability because it considers the most sensitive conditions of well operations. The methodology to develop MTs for chronic lowering of groundwater levels was based on discussion with GSA staff, GSPAC and technical representatives, input received from interested stakeholders and the public through public meetings, through individual stakeholder input to various GSA representatives, and review of GSP regulations and DWR BMPs. The DWR Well Completion Report (WCR) database was reviewed to evaluate the locations and construction details of various types of wells, including domestic, irrigation, industrial, and public water supply wells. Domestic wells represent the well type most likely to be impacted by declining groundwater levels, because they tend to be the shallowest wells. Minimum thresholds were tentatively set at 31 RMS wells based on modeling results and professional judgement. The initial MTs in this GSP may be considered as preliminary values which may change based on monitoring and annual reporting of groundwater conditions. The methodologies used to establish MTs for chronic lowering of groundwater levels are summarized below: 1) Evaluate the DWR WCR database for the location and construction details of wells (as described above).2) Review available existing and likely future wells with regard to several variables/273 criteria (e.g., GSA ownership and/or access to well, known well construction details, preference for wells with several years of observed water levels, availability of recent water level data, good spatial distribution) and select appropriate RMS locations. The methodology to develop MTs for chronic groundwater level decline was based on discussion with GSA staff, GSPAC and technical representatives, input received from interested stakeholders and the public through public meetings, through individual stakeholder input to various GSA representatives, and review of GSP regulations and DWR BMPs.
&&&
9.5.1.2.2 Minimum Thresholds
 The MT for chronic lowering of groundwater levels is defined as the minimum static groundwater elevation observed historically in October at wells with more than 10 years of available data prior to 2015 or the inferred minimum static groundwater elevation between 2005 to 2014 (10 years prior to SGMA adoption), for wells that lack at least 10 years of observed data. For wells that lack 10 years of observed data, the MT is informed by available data and historical groundwater levels simulated by the Napa Valley Integrated Hydrologic Model (NVIHM). Figure 9-3 shows the RMS network for chronic lowering of groundwater levels where an MT is in place at each site. Details for each RMS and its MTs, MOs, and IMs are outlined in Table 9-1. Details regarding MOs and IMs for chronic lowering of groundwater levels are described in Section 9.5.1.3. Further details of the chronic lowering of groundwater elevation RMS network are described in Section 9.5.1.4.
&&&
9.5.1.2.6 Impact on Beneficial Uses and Users
Groundwater level MTs are likely to have several effects on beneficial uses, users, land use, and property owners. Those expected to be impacted include agricultural land use and users,
urban/municipal land use and users, domestic land use and users, and ecological land use and users. RMS were selected to provide a basis for evaluating changes and impacts to the different uses and users of water wells throughout the Subbasin. 
Public Water Systems land use and users: Areas, uses, and users specified in this group include municipal water systems, small community water systems, and urban land uses. The chronic lowering of groundwater level MTs are set so that municipal and small community water systems can meet existing and projected water demands by protecting their ability to meet existing and projected demands through typical well and pumping configurations (e.g., depths, perforation intervals, pumping lifts).Self-Supplied land use and users: This group excludes agricultural related land uses and users, and only includes residential, commercial, and industrial water users that do not meet minimum requirements for designation as a public water system, as well as domestic well owners and other small scale surface water and recycled water users. The chronic lowering of groundwater level MTs protect self-supplied users of groundwater shortages by considering the depths to which wells are completed and protection of reasonable operating margins for available pumping drawdown.
&&&
9.5.1.2.7 Domestic Well Vulnerability Assessment
Impacts to production wells resulting from groundwater conditions can include damage to pumps resulting from cavitation or air entrainment at the pump intake, reductions in well production rates, and complete loss of production due to dewatering of the entire well. Impacts can begin to occur as groundwater levels approach or exceed the level of the pump intake. Domestic wells that supply household water demands for self-supplied groundwater users, or users not served by a public water system, are more likely to be the shallowest production wells in the Subbasin (Section 2) and therefore more vulnerable to groundwater level declines than other production wells. Evaluation of domestic well vulnerability to groundwater level declines is often limited by incomplete information on the construction of production wells, specifically the depth at which well screens terminate, and the depth within the well where pump intakes are set. Well construction information is available from WCRs required to be completed by drillers and submitted to DWR and now publicly available through the California Natural Resources Agency Open Data Portal. Information on the depth at which pumps are installed in wells is not reported on WCRs, may be changed independent of well construction, and presents a considerable data gap in basins throughout the state for well vulnerability assessments. Nevertheless, using best available information on domestic well construction in the Subbasin and potential vulnerability to groundwater level decline was evaluated using 50-year future projections of monthly groundwater levels developed using the NVIHM. The projections include Subbasin conditions simulated using both historical hydrologic conditions from 1969 to 2019 and projected conditions through 2070 that directly include best available projections for climate change in the Napa Valley Subbasin and Napa River Watershed. The vulnerability assessment was conducted to be inclusive of the 1,452 domestic wells known or inferred to be present within the Subbasin (Section 2). Consistent with other similar assessments, the determination of vulnerability was made on the basis of known well construction information using available WCRs, Napa County Assessor’s parcel data, available land use mapping, and inferred pump intake depth settings, 366 using methods derived from Pauloo and others (2020). The assessment was conducted on the basis of ten spatial analysis units spanning the entire Subbasin. Spatial analysis units were delineated to differentiate areas where groundwater levels are known or anticipated to be substantially similar (Figure 9-4). Delineations reflect the distribution of geologic formations in the Subbasin, the thickness of alluvial deposits, the location of significant hydrologic and geologic features in the Subbasin (including the East Napa Fault Zone, West Napa Fault Zone, and the Napa River), and observed, historical groundwater level data. Well construction information was summarized for each spatial analysis unit using WCR data available from DWR. Depths to the deepest well screen interval (or the depth at which perforated segments of the well casing terminate) were tabulated for all known or inferred domestic wells in each spatial analysis unit and exceedance percentiles calculated. The depth of pump intake placement was estimated using an offset relative to the deepest well screen interval, with the assumption that the well screen extends to the bottom of the well to maximize groundwater production. Based on input from engineers experienced in well design and construction and domestic well vulnerability evaluations by Pauloo et al (2020), estimated pump intake depths typically range from 30 to 40 feet above the bottom of the deepest well screen intervals. The pump intake depth is a key characteristic in evaluating domestic well vulnerability to groundwater levels. Vulnerable wells are defined as wells that may experience a loss in function due to sufficiently low water levels above the estimated pump intake depth, where a sufficient groundwater level above the pump intake is required for the pump to function properly. Two separate cases were evaluated: 1) a 30-foot offset; and 2) a 40-foot offset. For example, a domestic well with a bottom perforated depth of 100 feet is assumed to have the pump placed at a depth of 70 feet under the 30-foot offset scenario, and at a depth of 60 feet under the 40-foot offset scenario. The pump depth of domestic wells included in the assessment are summarized below in Table 9-2 for the 30-foot offset and Table 9-3 for the 40-foot offset. Domestic well vulnerability to potential future groundwater level declines 396 was evaluated by comparing projected groundwater levels at 31 RMS in the Subbasin. Projected water levels were simulated under 3 different 50-year scenarios (Section 8): 1) Scenario A- baseline historical hydrology; 2) Scenario B399 warmer and wetter hydrology; and 3) Scenario C- warmer and drought prone hydrology. Potential domestic well impacts were conservatively estimated to occur when projected water levels fall below the 30-foot and 40-foot offset above the bottom of the well perforations. A summary of the number of domestic wells in the 5th percentile for well depth where simulated groundwater levels exceeded the estimated pump intake depth is shown in Table 9-4 for each 50-year scenario.For each of the 3 climate scenarios tested, simulated groundwater levels 406 at the RMS wells show just one analysis unit as having groundwater levels below the 5th percentile pump depth under a 40-foot offset configuration. Groundwater level exceedances with respect to a 40-foot offset for pump depth occur under the following conditions for each scenario: Scenario A: Seven domestic wells in the 5th percentile pump depth group under a 40-foot offset experience some degree of vulnerability to lowered groundwater levels during dry years. Groundwater levels are typically no more than 5 feet below the 40-foot offset level during this time and occur seasonally during the fall in dry years. From 2027 to 2028 groundwater declines up to 10 feet below the 40-foot offset level. Scenario B: Seven domestic wells in the 5th percentile pump depth group under a 40-foot offset experience some degree of vulnerability to lowered groundwater levels during dry years. Groundwater levels are typically no more than 5 feet below the 40-foot offset level and occur seasonally during the fall in dry years. Scenario C: Seven domestic wells in the 5th percentile pump depth group under a 40-foot offset experience some degree of vulnerability to lowered groundwater levels during dry years. Groundwater levels are typically no more than 10 feet below the 40-foot offset level and occur seasonally during the  fall in dry years. Exceedances become more common towards the end of the simulation period in 2070.
&&&
The minimum threshold for degraded water quality shall be the degradation of water quality, including the migration of contaminant plumes that impair water supplies or other indicator of water quality as determined by the Agency that may lead to undesirable results. The minimum threshold shall be based on the number of supply wells, a volume of water, or a location of an isocontour that exceeds concentrations of constituents determined by the Agency to be of concern for the basin. In setting minimum thresholds for degraded water quality, the Agency shall consider local, state, and federal water quality standards applicable to the basin.

9.5.4.2.1 Methodology
Information used to establish the MT for degraded groundwater quality includes: • Historical groundwater quality from basin‐wide monitoring wells in the Subbasin.  • Depths and locations of existing wells. • Federal and state drinking water quality standards. • Information from interested parties of significant and unreasonable conditions.  Section 6 provides a description of groundwater and surface water conditions that were evaluated to  understand the temporal and spatial trends of the aquifers and streams in the Napa Valley Subbasin.  Groundwater quality constituents of most concern to beneficial users in the Subbasin were collected  from a number of federal, state, and local sources. Section 6 characterizes historical and current  groundwater quality conditions and identifies groundwater quality constituents that may affect  beneficial uses and users of groundwater. There are various water quality concerns to recognize in the  Napa Valley Subbasin, including naturally occurring constituents such as arsenic and constituents  including nitrate that are typically associated with land uses. Groundwater quality in the Subbasin on the whole is generally of good quality for irrigation and adequate for domestic purposes including drinking water and other uses; however, there are areas of poorer water quality within the Subbasin. These groundwater quality conditions are more likely to affect domestic, public supply, and agricultural well  users and potentially environmental users of groundwater. Potential effects of degraded water quality on domestic wells and public supply wells in the Subbasin include costs associated with treatment of raw water, securing an alternate source of supply to meet drinking water standards. Degraded groundwater quality can also negatively affect the crops and environmental  users of groundwater by  impairing growth and development, which may result in reduced crop yields or reductions in suitable habitat.
&&&
9.5.4.2.6 Minimum Threshold Impact on Beneficial Uses and Users
Public Water Systems land use and users: Groundwater quality MTs are set so that municipal and small community water systems can meet existing and projected water demands by protecting from future groundwater quality degradation. Depending on the water system, degraded groundwater quality could increase treatment costs, costs associated with maintaining treatment systems, and possibly render small public water systems unviable if treatment systems are not present.
Self-Supplied land use and users: The groundwater quality MTs protect domestic users of groundwater by applying drinking water standards.

For wells that lack 10 years of observed data, the MT is informed by available data and historical groundwater levels simulated by the Napa Valley Integrated Hydrologic Model (NVIHM). Figure 9-3 shows the RMS network for chronic lowering of groundwater levels where an MT is in place at each site. Details for each RMS and its MTs, MOs, and IMs are outlined in Table 9-1. Details regarding MOs and IMs for chronic lowering of groundwater levels are described in Section 9.5.1.3. Further details of the chronic lowering of groundwater elevation RMS network are described in Section 9.5.1.4.
&&&
9.5.1.2.6 Impact on Beneficial Uses and Users
Groundwater level MTs are likely to have several effects on beneficial uses, users, land use, and property owners. Those expected to be impacted include agricultural land use and users,urban/municipal land use and users, domestic land use and users, and ecological land use and users. RMS were selected to provide a basis for evaluating changes and impacts to the different uses and users of water wells throughout the Subbasin. 
Native Vegetation land use and users: Environmental uses include groundwater dependent ecosystems, for which data gaps have been identified and new monitoring installations planned, and recreation. Initially, a baseline will be established to provide a basis for identifying effects of chronic lowering of groundwater and setting protective MTs. In the case that groundwater levels drop to MTs for chronic lowering of groundwater levels, it could lead to a significant and unreasonable reduction of flow of groundwater toward streams, which could adversely affect ecological habitats.
&&&
9.5.1.2.8 Groundwater Dependent Ecosystems Vulnerability Assessment
Impacts to GDEs as a result of chronic decline of groundwater levels can include altering the extent and quality of riparian and instream habitats by reducing access to groundwater for vegetation and altering flow regimes necessary for spawning/rearing habitat for fish. For a full description of GDEs and other species of special concern refer to Sections 3 and 6 of this GSP. Under SGMA, undesirable results occur
when significant and unreasonable effects are caused by groundwater conditions. To be consistent with this SGMA provision and guidance from The Nature Conservancy (TNC), this assessment assumes that if  there is little to no change in groundwater conditions from baseline conditions, then there are likely no  significant and unreasonable effects for the corresponding sustainability indicator. This assumption is
supported by data presented in Section 6 that documents stable to increasing GDE vegetation conditions throughout the Subbasin based on available data from 1985 through 2018. In this context, baseline conditions are defined as conditions occurring from 2005 to 2015, which includes a very dry year in 2007 and dry to below normal period from 2012 to 2015. Effects of groundwater elevations on interconnected surface waters is evaluated for the interconnected surface water sustainability indicator described in Section 9.5.6. The MTs for chronic lowering of groundwater levels, the sustainability indicator most likely to affect GDE vegetation, are based on selection of RMS from the groundwater level monitoring network within the Subbasin. For this assessment of GDE vulnerability, groundwater levels at 8 RMS for chronic lowering of groundwater levels were evaluated alongside estimated maximum rooting depths for phreatophytes mapped within a half mile of each RMS. The eight RMS considered for this evaluation include five existing and three planned shallow monitoring wells located along riparian habitats in the Subbasin.  Spatial mapping of GDEs presented in this section incorporate TNC’s Natural Communities Commonly
Associated with Groundwater (NCCAG) Dataset and Napa County’s recently  updated vegetation map dataset (Thorne et al., 2019). Plants access groundwater through their roots, making the depths that their roots reach below ground useful in determining whether GDEs are susceptible to potential effects due to changes in groundwater conditions (e.g., groundwater levels, surface water depletions). Although reported rooting depths vary across California for the same species due to site-specific conditions,maximum rooting depth data for phreatophytes was acquired from TNC’s Plant Rooting Depth Database4 and used as a reference for determining GDE vegetation vulnerability. If groundwater levels  fall outside of the historical range, plants can be adversely impacted. Depths greater than the historical
 range could prevent plants from accessing needed groundwater, while depths shallower than the historical range, if occurring over a prolonged period, can induce stress and lead to root damage.  In addition to utilizing maximum plant rooting depths, available vegetation metric time-series data provided by TNC through the GDE Pulse5 web map are evaluated for evidence of biological response to  changing groundwater levels. Vegetation metrics for Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) and  Normalized Difference Moisture Index (NDMI) are used to estimate vegetation greenness and  vegetation moisture. Vegetation with higher NDVI values indicate increased density of chlorophyll and  photosynthetic capacity in the canopy, an indicator of vigorous, growing vegetation. Similarly, high  NDMI values indicate that the vegetation canopy has high water content and is therefore not drought  stressed. A linear regression analysis was conducted on NDVI, NDMI, and precipitation data compiled  from 1985 to 2018 to evaluate the potential relationships between precipitation and vegetation health.  No strong relationship was found for any of the GDEs between vegetation health and precipitation,  which can be interpreted as a weak coupling between precipitation and NDVI/NDMI. Therefore, groundwater conditions can be assumed as the stronger influence to changes in NDVI and NDMI.  Available time series data of vegetation indices in the vicinity of RMS are provided in Section 6.
 Five of the eight RMS in this assessment are existing monitoring wells in the Subbasin constructed for  tracking shallow (~30-50 feet) groundwater levels in tandem with stream levels. The remaining three  RMS are planned for construction in 2021/2022 where the groundwater levels and MTs presented for  these sites are based on simulated conditions from the NVIHM that consider planned construction  details and location. Starting from the northernmost RMS and moving south, an assessment of  groundwater conditions and potential impacts to GDEs at each of the eight RMS is provided below, with  monitoring results provided in Appendix 9A. Table 9-5 lists the groundwater level RMS evaluated.
&&&
Depletion of interconnected surface waters: It is recognized that shallow groundwater and surface water are interconnected in portions of the Napa Valley Subbasin. Changes in groundwater elevation could impact GDE areas as a result in decreased outflow of fresh groundwater due to chronic water level declines. Minimum thresholds for depletion of interconnected surface waters at RMS in shallow alluvial sediments are based on more than 10 years of available data or, if not available, available data supplemented by simulated historical groundwater levels. The groundwater level MTs established for the Subbasin avoid undesirable results from depletion of interconnected surface waters by maintaining groundwater elevations within the historical levels. Therefore, groundwater level MTs will not induce additional depletion of interconnected surface waters and will not result in a significant or unreasonable depletion of interconnected surface waters.
&&&
9.5.6.2 Minimum Thresholds, Measurable Objectives, and Interim Milestones Section 354.28(c)(6) of the SGMA regulations states:
The minimum threshold for depletions of interconnected surface water shall be the rate or volume of surface water depletions caused by groundwater use that has adverse impacts on beneficial uses of the surface water and may lead to undesirable results. The minimum threshold established for depletions of interconnected surface water shall be supported by the following: (A) The location, quantity, and timing of depletions of interconnected surface water. (B) A description of the groundwater and surface water model used to quantify surface water depletion. If a numerical groundwater and surface water model is not used to quantify surface water depletion, the Plan shall identify and describe an equally effective method, tool, or analytical model to accomplish the requirements of this Paragraph.
9.5.6.2.1 Methodology
Information used to establish the MTs for interconnected surface waters include: • Historical groundwater elevations within the Subbasin. • Depths and locations of existing wells. • Current and historical groundwater elevation contour maps. • Modeling scenarios for Subbasin sustainable yield including climate change. Streamflow and stream stage data collected by the Napa County Flood Control and Water Conservation District (NCFCWCD) and the USGS. • Past hydrologic reports, monitoring reports, and case studies. • Other Information from the GSA and interested parties regarding significant and unreasonable conditions.
&&&
9.5.6.2.4 Quantitative Measurement of Minimum Threshold
The groundwater level MTs for interconnected surface waters will be directly and quantitatively measured at each groundwater level RMS by measuring the depth to groundwater from a surveyed reference point. Groundwater level monitoring will be conducted in accordance with the monitoring plan and protocols outlined in Section 5 and will meet the requirements of the technical and reporting standards included in the GSP regulations. Stream depletion MTs for interconnected surface waters are quantified using the NVIHM. The NVIHM accounts for a multitude of hydrologic data such as precipitation, groundwater extraction, groundwater levels, stream discharge, and local hydrogeologic conditions to quantify the volume of streamflow depletion during Summer/early Fall (June to October) of a given year. The approach used to quantify stream depletion is centered around comparing the calibrated model (which includes pumping for irrigation) to a hypothetical scenario where groundwater pumping for irrigation of agriculture and landscaping is removed from the model.
&&&
9.5.6.2.6 Impact on Beneficial Uses and Users
Native Vegetation land use and users: Interconnected surface water MTs were specifically established to protect aquatic species and GDEs present in streams connected to groundwater. Groundwater levels within the MTs for this sustainability indicator increase the overall hydraulic gradient of groundwater to streams, therefore allowing greater groundwater flow into the stream.

Remote Sensing. Detailed in TNC’s report GDE Pulse: Taking the Pulse 1352 of Groundwater Dependent Ecosystems with Satellite Data, remotely sensed satellite data from NASA’s Landsat Program can be applied to monitor the health of vegetation worldwide (Klausmeyer et al., 2019). Landsat imagery is made available at a resolution of 30 meters every 16 days, in which long-term temporal trends of vegetation metrics are made available on TNC’s GDE Pulse web app, allowing users to infer the relationships between groundwater levels, precipitation, and GDE vegetation metrics. Detailed in the report, the methods in which TNC processed the satellite data results in a geospatial representation of the Normalized Derived Vegetation Index (NDVI) to estimate vegetation greenness and Normalized Derived Moisture Index (NDMI) to estimate vegetation moisture. TNC provides the average NDVI and NDMI for all Landsat pixels, masked to spatial data from the iGDE database, to present the average and trend geospatial layers representing positive and negative trends in the two vegetation metrics.

Biologic Field Assessments. In addition to monitoring potential changes in the occurrence and distribution of GDEs within the Subbasin, biological assessments are necessary to provide detailed site information for evaluating the potential effects on GDEs, investigating stream habitat conditions, and evaluating the status of rare, threatened, or endangered species. The California Surface Water Ambient Monitoring Program’s (SWAMP) Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) for the Collection of Field Data for Bioassessments of California Wadeable Streams: Benthic Macroinvertebrates, Algae, and Physical Habitat provides methods for assessing the ecological conditions of wadeable streams and rivers based on the composition of the benthic macroinvertebrate and benthic algal assemblages (Ode, 2016). The procedures also produce standardized measurements of instream and riparian habitat and ambient water chemistry to support interpretation of the biological data.

11.4.1 Project #1: Managed Aquifer Recharge
11.4.1.1 Project Overview 
Water supply augmentation through managed aquifer recharge (MAR) enhances aquifer replenishment with the potential to mitigate undesirable results particularly during periods of drought. Managed aquifer recharge can occur through active approaches such as recharge ponds and injection wells or more passive approaches such as intentionally inundating riparian corridors or agricultural lands during periods of high streamflow (flood-MAR or AgMAR). The planned project involving managed aquifer recharge, and approved by the GSPAC, is summarized in Table 11-5. The implementation of managed aquifer recharge was first simulated in the NVIHM and determined to be a suitable PMA for the Subbasin. This project would involve diverting high flow in tributaries during wet years and spreading over vacant parcels and agricultural fields. Managed aquifer recharge is intended to increase groundwater storage and increase streamflow and groundwater uptake from plants during wet years.

11.8.3 Watershed Information and Conservation Council (WICC) 
The Napa County Board of Supervisors passed Resolution No. 02-103 on May 21, 2002, creating the  Watershed Information & Conservation Council (WICC). The WICC was chosen to represent the diversity of Napa County’s community. The WICC’s mission is: To improve the health of Napa County’s watersheds by informing, engaging, and fostering partnerships 973 within the community.The role of the WICC is to assist the County Board of Supervisors in their decision-making process and serve as a conduit for citizen input by gathering, analyzing, and recommending options related to the management of watershed resources countywide. The WICC has a responsibility to publicly evaluate and discuss matters relating to watershed restoration and resource protection activities, coordination of land acquisition, development of long-term watershed resource management plans and programs. The WICC also serves to provide public outreach and education, monitoring and assessment coordination, and data management of Napa County's water and watershed resources.
